name: Clean Commit History with Interactive Rebase
description: Squash, reorder, reword, and edit commits using interactive rebase to craft a clear, logical commit history before merging.
prerequisites: A feature branch with multiple commits
Understanding of commit history and HEAD references

steps.title: Review recent commit history
steps.description: Visualize the last 15 commits with a graph to understand the current history and decide how many commits to rebase.
steps.tip: Use --author to filter commits if working on a shared branch.

steps.title: Start interactive rebase
steps.description: Opens your editor with the last 5 commits. Each line starts with 'pick'. Change the action word to squash, reword, edit, drop, or fixup.
steps.warning: Never rebase commits that have already been pushed to a shared branch unless you coordinate with your team.

steps.title: Squash fixup commits into their parent
steps.command:
# In the editor, change 'pick' to 'fixup' for commits to squash silently
# Or use 'squash' to combine and edit the commit message
pick abc1234 feat: add user authentication
fixup def5678 fix typo in auth module
fixup ghi9012 add missing import
steps.description: Fixup merges a commit into the one above it and discards the fixup commit message. Squash does the same but lets you edit the combined message.
steps.tip: Use 'git commit --fixup=<hash>' while working to pre-mark commits for auto-squashing with 'git rebase -i --autosquash'.

steps.title: Reorder commits for logical grouping
steps.command:
# Simply rearrange lines in the editor:
pick abc1234 feat: add user model
pick jkl3456 feat: add user API routes
pick mno7890 feat: add user UI components
pick def5678 test: add user test suite
steps.description: Move lines up or down to reorder commits. Git will replay them in the new order. Conflicts may arise if commits depend on each other.

steps.title: Resolve any conflicts during rebase
steps.command:
git status
# Fix conflicts in listed files, then:
git add .
git rebase --continue
steps.description: If Git encounters conflicts while replaying commits, fix them, stage the resolutions, and continue the rebase.
steps.tip: Use 'git rebase --abort' at any point to cancel and return to the original state.

steps.title: Force push the cleaned branch
steps.description: Push the rewritten history. The --force-with-lease flag prevents overwriting commits pushed by others since your last fetch.
steps.warning: Always use --force-with-lease instead of --force. It is a safety net against accidentally overwriting a collaborator's work.

fullScript:
#!/usr/bin/env bash
# interactive-rebase-workflow.sh — Guided interactive rebase
set -euo pipefail

COMMIT_COUNT="${1:-5}"
BRANCH=$(git rev-parse --abbrev-ref HEAD)

echo "Current branch: $BRANCH"
echo ""
echo "=== Last $COMMIT_COUNT commits ==="
git log --oneline --graph -"$COMMIT_COUNT"

echo ""
echo "Rebase actions cheatsheet:"
echo "  pick   = keep commit as-is"
echo "  reword = keep commit, edit message"
echo "  squash = merge into previous, edit combined message"
echo "  fixup  = merge into previous, discard this message"
echo "  drop   = remove commit entirely"
echo "  edit   = pause after commit for amending"
echo ""
read -rp "Start interactive rebase for last $COMMIT_COUNT commits? (y/N) " confirm
if [[ "$confirm" =~ ^[Yy]$ ]]; then
  git rebase -i HEAD~"$COMMIT_COUNT"
  echo ""
  echo "Rebase complete. New history:"
  git log --oneline --graph -"$COMMIT_COUNT"
  echo ""
  read -rp "Push with --force-with-lease? (y/N) " push_confirm
  if [[ "$push_confirm" =~ ^[Yy]$ ]]; then
    git push --force-with-lease
    echo "Pushed successfully."
  fi
fi

faq.question: What is the difference between squash and fixup?
faq.answer: Both merge a commit into the one above it. Squash opens an editor to combine both commit messages, while fixup silently discards the fixup commit's message and keeps only the parent's.

faq.question: Can I undo an interactive rebase after completing it?
faq.answer: Yes. Use 'git reflog' to find the commit hash before the rebase, then 'git reset --hard <hash>' to restore the original history. This works as long as you have not garbage collected.

faq.question: How do I use autosquash to automate fixup commits?
faq.answer: When committing, use 'git commit --fixup=<target-hash>'. Then run 'git rebase -i --autosquash HEAD~N'. Git will automatically reorder fixup commits and mark them with the fixup action.
